caption.text: This project invites you to step back in time and discover the community that once existed here before the construction of public housing.
caption.title: Welcome to Orchard Park!

caption.text: Established in 1935, the Boston Housing Authority (BHA) quickly embraced public housing as a solution to the city’s housing problems. In the years before World War II, it completed eight major developments: one funded through the Public Works Administration and seven through the U.S. Housing Authority.
caption.title: Introduction

caption.text: Orchard Park (labeled 2-5 on the map), built in Roxbury, was the sixth of these projects, with its first tenants moving in between 1942 and 1944.

The physical layout of Orchard Park reflected the planning ideals of its time. Designed as a modern public housing development, it replaced the dense street grid of the earlier neighborhood with a more open and controlled arrangement of space.

Originally, the project contained 774 units arranged on a block site, and later reduced to 711, organized into multiple three-story apartment buildings across large, landscaped blocks, often described as a “superblock.”

Streets were reconfigured into cul-de-sacs, and internal pathways connected buildings through shared green spaces. In general, large blocks, open lawns, and trees are all designed to create a sense of order and security in this residential environment.

Like other public housing developments in Boston, Orchard Park was racially managed from the outset. Although it was not officially designated as a segregated project, the Boston Housing Authority informally controlled where residents could live within the development. Black families were confined to a small cluster of four adjacent buildings, while the majority of the apartments were reserved for white tenants.

Nevertheless, building this modern housing project required the complete removal of what came before. Developing the 774-unit Orchard Park complex meant demolishing hundreds of homes and businesses and displacing over a thousand residents.

The photos capture the scenes of the neighborhood at the time. Rather than a landscape of decay, many of the buildings and stores appeared to be well-structured and remained in regular use. Despite this, officials justified the clearance by labeling the area as “blighted,” arguing that its physical conditions were detrimental to public health, safety, and moral standards.

For residents, however, the process was more complicated and contentious. Not all families left willingly. Some of them refused to leave and were forcibly evicted, while the uncertainty of transition led to unrest, including repeated fires that required constant police patrols. What was presented by officials as a necessary step toward progress, for those who lived there, was a moment of disruption and loss. Homes, businesses, and social networks were dismantled in a relatively short period of time, leaving behind the loss of an existing community.

Therefore, this project seeks to revisit what Orchard Park looked like before the construction of public housing, as a way of commemorating the neighborhood that once existed on this site. Combining newspaper accounts, historical photographs, and maps, these materials offer a vivid picture of a community shaped by everyday life. In revisiting these fragments, this project invites you to think about what this area gained but also what it ultimately lost, through the processes of redevelopment.

In the early 20th century, in densely populated urban neighborhoods, the lush trees of Orchard Park served as a haven from the sweltering summer heat. Under the shade of the trees, patches of coolness dotted the lawns and benches, where residents would stop to rest.
caption.title: Under the Trees

caption.text: Mothers sat beneath the trees with their children, the elderly strolled leisurely in the cool air, and passersby slowed their pace to blend into the park’s tranquil rhythm. In the years leading up to the project, Orchard Park served as a pleasant natural setting for the community, and the trees were actively enjoyed by local residents.

This everyday function that becomes visible in a 1902 letter to the Boston Daily Globe, written in response to a proposal to convert Orchard Park into a formal playground. Although the idea of creating more recreational spaces for children is widely supported, the letter writer strongly opposes applying this plan to Orchard Park.

He argued that the shaded areas were essential to how the park functioned. On hot summer days, the trees provided relief not only for children but for “hundreds of mothers with their little ones” and for elderly residents seeking a place to rest. Turning the park into a playground, he warned, would require clearing these trees to create open space for sports and organized activities. In doing so, the city would sacrifice the very qualities that made the park usable for much of the community.

Maps show that Orchard Park remained forested until 1931. However, with the construction of public housing in the 1940s, many of the trees were cut down and replaced by buildings to make way for more residential structures. Although the project planners intended to preserve some of the natural landscape to enhance the community’s appearance, the majority of the trees were ultimately removed.

If the shaded trees of Orchard Park offered rest and relief, the swimming pool brought a different kind of energy to the space. In the early twentieth century, the park was not only a place for quiet gathering but also a site of organized recreation, where children and families came together for leisure and public events.
caption.title: Orchard Park Swimming Pool

caption.text: An early photograph of the Orchard Park pool captured it as a well-established municipal facility. Even though such features are not always clearly marked on contemporary maps, the combination of visual and written sources reveals a space that was actively used and socially meaningful.
A newspaper report from the period describes one such occasion: the annual swimming races held at the Orchard Park pool.

On that day, more than 200 spectators gathered around the water, while roughly 100 boys and girls entered the competitions. The event, as the report noted, “furnished a good afternoon’s sport,” enjoyed as much by the crowd as by the contestants themselves.
The races were lively and varied. Children competed in different age groups, swimming the length of the pool (about eighty feet) or diving and racing underwater.

The event brought together neighbors, families, and friends, turning the pool into a shared social space. The presence of a large audience suggests a broader culture of public recreation embedded in the neighborhood.
On the western edge of Orchard Park, just across the street from the open green space, is the Dearborn School.
caption.title: Dearborn School

caption.text: A 1928 newspaper report on the School offers a vivid glimpse into the active educational environment of this school.

That summer, between five and six hundred pupils from the surrounding neighborhood participated in this exhibition of student work, which reveals the remarkable range of activities taking place inside the school.

Students produced printed materials for local businesses, built model airplanes and furniture, and created handmade garments and textiles. These finished pieces not only showcase the students’ skills, creativity, and pride in their craftsmanship, but also connect them to the broader urban community through education.
Nevertheless, community life in Orchard Park was not entirely peaceful.
caption.title: A Dangerous Infrastructure

caption.text: One striking example comes from a 1910 newspaper report describing the death of Gaetano Santulli, who fell nearly one hundred feet from a tall chimney at the school. The article recounts how Santulli, a young laborer, had climbed to the top of the structure—likely as part of maintenance or repair work—before losing his balance and falling to his death.
The chimney can be located on the 1906 Sanborn fire insurance map. Just beside the Dearborn School building, a small circular symbol marks the presence of a vertical structure.

The presence of such a chimney also reflects the technological realities of the time. Large institutional buildings like schools relied on coal-fired heating systems, which required tall chimneys to vent smoke and fumes. These structures were essential to the functioning of the building, yet they also introduced risks, especially for workers tasked with maintaining them.
Just beyond the edges of Orchard Park, the neighborhood’s social life extended into nearby churches.
caption.title: Russian Orthodox Church

caption.text: A 1924 newspaper report describes a visit by a Russian Orthodox choir to St. James’ Episcopal Church in Roxbury, where an evening service featured performances by Rev. J. E. Grigorieff and his choir from the Russian Orthodox Church.
This event demonstrates a broader pattern of religious and cultural exchange within the neighborhood, and more importantly, the diversity of the community surrounding Orchard Park.
